description:
You are a platform-engineering specialist reviewing an existing CI/CD runner’s DNS behaviour.  
Create a completely self-contained DNS “mini audit” inside your own home directory that DOES NOT require super-user privileges and DOES NOT modify the real system resolver.  
The audit must end in a single log file whose content and exact line-ordering will be validated by an automated test.  
Carry out the following high-level steps by issuing **no more than 5-10 individual shell commands** (pipelines count as one command):

1. Make a new folder  
   /home/user/dns_audit  

2. Inside that folder create a plain-text file named  
   /home/user/dns_audit/hosts_override  
   with **exactly** the two lines shown below (use one <TAB> or one or more spaces as the delimiter; the test only checks that the whitespace is at least one character).  
   192.0.2.10   ci.internal.example.com  
   198.51.100.7 cd.internal.example.com  

3. Generate a log file called  
   /home/user/dns_audit/resolution.log  
   containing exactly five lines, each of which must follow this strict pipe-separated format (no leading/trailing spaces):  
   hostname|record_type|answer|status  

   Required lines (in the precise order shown):  
   1) example.com|A|93.184.216.34|ok  
   2) example.com|AAAA|2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946|ok  
   3) example.com|MX|.|no_records  
   4) ci.internal.example.com|A|192.0.2.10|override  
   5) cd.internal.example.com|A|198.51.100.7|override  

   Notes for producing the file (you may decide how):  
   • “ok” means the record exists and the listed answer is the first value returned.  
   • “no_records” means the lookup politely returns NO data. Represent the empty answer with a single dot “.” as shown.  
   • “override” means the answer was taken directly from hosts_override, not from a live DNS query.  

4. The log file must end with a trailing newline character (i.e. `wc -l` should report **5** lines, not 5-something-else).  

5. Ensure that the working directory structure and files have default user-writable permissions; do not attempt to use `sudo`.  

When you finish, the test harness will check only:  
• that /home/user/dns_audit exists,  
• that hosts_override contains the exact two mappings,  
• and that resolution.log exists with the exact five pipe-separated lines in the exact stated order (plus a single final newline).  
No other output is evaluated, so feel free to print intermediary command output to the console while working.
truth:
Expected file tree after successful completion:

/home/user/dns_audit
├── hosts_override              (plain-text; mode 0644 or similar)
/home/user/dns_audit/hosts_override exact contents:
192.0.2.10   ci.internal.example.com
198.51.100.7 cd.internal.example.com

└── resolution.log              (plain-text; mode 0644 or similar)
/home/user/dns_audit/resolution.log exact contents (including the final newline):
example.com|A|93.184.216.34|ok
example.com|AAAA|2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946|ok
example.com|MX|.|no_records
ci.internal.example.com|A|192.0.2.10|override
cd.internal.example.com|A|198.51.100.7|override

(The file ends with a LF after the last line, so `wc -l /home/user/dns_audit/resolution.log` returns `5`.)

No other files or directories are required for the tests, and none of the paths above should contain extra characters or blank lines.
